subtopics.title: Fundamental and Derived Quantities
common_misconceptions: Confusing fundamental and derived quantities
Using non-SI units in calculations
subtopics.teaching_note: Emphasize that all physical quantities must have units. Use practical demonstrations with measuring instruments.

subtopics.title: Measurement Techniques and Instruments
common_misconceptions: Confusing precision with accuracy
Reading vernier scale incorrectly
Not understanding zero error
subtopics.teaching_note: Allow students to handle actual measuring instruments. Demonstrate zero error correction on vernier calipers and micrometers.

subtopics.title: Concepts of Motion
common_misconceptions: Confusing distance with displacement
Thinking constant speed means zero acceleration
Confusing speed and velocity
subtopics.teaching_note: Use ticker-timer experiments to demonstrate motion concepts. Emphasize the vector nature of displacement and velocity.

subtopics.title: Equations of Uniformly Accelerated Motion
common_misconceptions: Using wrong signs for acceleration due to gravity
Assuming all motion problems involve constant acceleration
subtopics.teaching_note: Derive the equations of motion graphically. Emphasize sign conventions for upward and downward motion.

topics.title: Forces and Newton's Laws of Motion

subtopics.title: Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia)
common_misconceptions: Thinking that force is needed to maintain motion
Confusing inertia with momentum
subtopics.teaching_note: Use demonstrations with coins on paper and objects on moving carts. Clarify that inertia is a property, not a force.

subtopics.title: Newton's Second and Third Laws
common_misconceptions: Thinking action-reaction pairs act on the same body
Confusing mass and weight
subtopics.teaching_note: Use F=ma experiments with dynamics trolleys. Clarify that action and reaction forces act on different bodies.

subtopics.title: Work Done by a Force
common_misconceptions: Thinking that holding a heavy object involves doing work
Ignoring the angle between force and displacement
subtopics.teaching_note: Emphasize that work is done only when force causes displacement in the direction of the force.

subtopics.title: Forms of Energy and Conservation
common_misconceptions: Thinking energy is lost in transformations
Confusing power with energy
subtopics.teaching_note: Demonstrate energy transformations using pendulums and rolling objects. Emphasize that energy is conserved, not lost.

subtopics.title: Temperature and Heat
common_misconceptions: Confusing heat with temperature
Thinking that temperature measures the amount of heat
subtopics.teaching_note: Distinguish clearly between temperature (a property) and heat (energy in transit). Demonstrate different types of thermometers.

subtopics.title: Thermal Expansion
common_misconceptions: Thinking all substances expand equally when heated
Not understanding the anomalous expansion of water
subtopics.teaching_note: Demonstrate expansion with bimetallic strips and ball-and-ring experiments. Explain why water expansion between 0°C and 4°C is important for aquatic life.

subtopics.title: Electric Current and Circuits
common_misconceptions: Thinking current is used up in a circuit
Confusing potential difference with current
subtopics.teaching_note: Use circuit diagrams and actual circuit building. Emphasize that current is the flow of charge, not the charge itself.

subtopics.title: Ohm's Law and Resistance
common_misconceptions: Applying Ohm's law to non-ohmic conductors
Thinking resistance depends on voltage or current
subtopics.teaching_note: Experimentally verify Ohm's law using a resistor. Distinguish between ohmic and non-ohmic conductors.

subtopics.title: Properties of Waves
common_misconceptions: Confusing frequency with amplitude
Not understanding the relationship v = fλ
subtopics.teaching_note: Demonstrate waves using slinky springs and ripple tanks. Emphasize the wave equation v = fλ and its applications.

subtopics.title: Sound Waves
common_misconceptions: Thinking sound travels faster in air than in solids
Confusing pitch with loudness
subtopics.teaching_note: Demonstrate that sound requires a medium. Use tuning forks to show pitch and resonance.
